title: NFS Mount Stale Handle Detection
spl:
index=os sourcetype=syslog "nfs" ("stale" OR "stale NFS file handle" OR "Stale NFS")
| stats count by host, nfs_server
| where count > 0
description: Stale NFS handles cause application hangs and I/O failures that severely impact users.
value: Stale NFS handles cause application hangs and I/O failures that severely impact users.
implementation: Monitor kernel logs and NFS client logs for stale handle errors. Create immediate alerts with escalation to storage team. Add search to identify affected processes and suggest remount or NFS server recovery.

references.title: Splunk Lantern ŌĆö use case library

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`.
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=syslog, kernel NFS logs`.
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
Monitor kernel logs and NFS client logs for stale handle errors. Create immediate alerts with escalation to storage team. Add search to identify affected processes and suggest remount or NFS server recovery.

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=syslog "nfs" ("stale" OR "stale NFS file handle" OR "Stale NFS")
| stats count by host, nfs_server
| where count > 0
```

Understanding this SPL

**NFS Mount Stale Handle Detection** ŌĆö Stale NFS handles cause application hangs and I/O failures that severely impact users.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=syslog, kernel NFS logs`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: syslog. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=syslog. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
ŌĆó `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, nfs_server** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
ŌĆó Filters the current rows with `where count > 0` ŌĆö typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
On the host, compare with `top`, `htop`, `vmstat`, `iostat`, or `sar` as appropriate to this use case. For log-only detections, compare with the relevant file under `/var/log` (or `journalctl`) on a test host. Confirm that indexed event counts and field values line up with what you see on the system and that your role can search the right indexes and fields.

Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Alert, Table
grandmaExplanation: Stale NFS handles cause application hangs and I/O failures that severely impact users ŌĆö so you find out before users do when something is slowing down or breaking.
knownFalsePositives: NFS blips during network or storage work look like stale handles in logs. Confirm app-level I/O errors, not a single message.
